next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 4

The coaching suite smelled like adhesive tape and the faint metallic discharge of processing hardware running warm. Margot got there fifteen minutes before Tomás's session, which was ten minutes early. Professionalism, she told herself, meant checking the monitoring displays, running a diagnostic on the feed interface, and reviewing the hurdle and four-hundred targets. All of that was true.

But she also sat in the coaching rig with the wrist mount already docked, waiting for the signal handshake that could not begin until Tomás came in and connected on his end, and watched the green ready-light blink until her mouth went dry.

The suite sat beside the indoor track, cut off from the training floor by a glass observation window. Three monitors on the wall held empty biometric grids. The chair was padded and built for long sessions. It was the most comfortable chair in the building. Margot hated that she knew that.

Tomás came through the door at 9:02 in training kit, hurdle spikes in one hand, water bottle in the other. The spikes were custom-fitted and Meridian-issued. He carried them carefully, still a little amazed by them, like they might stop belonging to him if he gripped too hard. He was smiling. He usually was.

"Morning, Coach."

"Morning. You eat?"

"Yeah, eggs and that quinoa stuff from the cafeteria. It's not — how do you say — it's not bad, actually. My mom makes better, but." He shrugged. "She makes better than everything, so."

"Sit down. Let me check your calibration readings before we start."

He sat across from her. The auxiliary cable clicked...
open_hooks_to_carry: Yusuf transfers raw biometric files to a personal encrypted archive
Video call with Farah in Birmingham—she is cheerful, deflecting questions about her symptoms, her speech slightly slower than last month
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ont to Basel. A delay at Laufen. An apology for arriving late to something the letter never bothered to specify. The dead aunt had kept it for reasons the novel hadn't explained yet. Not understanding that felt ordinary again, almost pleasant.

Three more pages went by without trouble. Comprehension returned so completely that it became invisible. Room, bed, book, page, all of it joined up the way it ordinarily did.

At lights-out the bedside lamp clicked off and the wall display shifted into night mode, ticker slowed to a pulse, green reflections moving faintly across the ceiling. Lena rolled onto one elbow and woke the screen again.

A symptom menu opened. Dizziness. Nausea. Muscular pain. Loss of consciousness.

She read the list twice. Nothing on it matched twenty-one minutes upright in bed with her eyes open and no memory attached. If she flagged loss of consciousness, the overnight log would show otherwise. If she left a note, it would be only her saying it had happened.

She closed the menu.

Yusuf was already going to inspect the turn. Margot already had the morning anomaly in the system. She was not going to report this. Until there was a trace to point at, it would sound like panic, or poor sleep, or a language failure. She did not want to hear any of those explanations in someone else's voice.

She tapped the brightness back up until the desk, radiator, and book on the blanket came back into view. Then she lay flat on her back, two fingers resting over the warm disc at the base of her skull, and kept her eyes on the seconds in the top corner as long as she could.

setups_to_plant.description: Yusuf maintains a personal encrypted data archive on a device in his quarters, separate from Kirchberg's systems
Yusuf's sister Farah has Voss-Kessler syndrome, progressive neural deterioration; the data he collects from athletes may contribute to treatment research

state_deltas_required: Yusuf's dual obligation—to the athletes he calibrates and to his sister whose treatment depends on data that may soon be locked away
The engineer's hidden project becomes visible to the reader; the data he needs is about to become corporate property
